ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term specific to kulfi exists; mapped to broader frozen dessert food product class (FOODON_03311069).
ontology.foodex2_description: Ice cream and similar frozen dairy desserts
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code specific to kulfi; mapped to closest category A00LC (ice cream and similar frozen dairy desserts) as kulfi is a dense frozen dairy product.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for kulfi as a specific food concept; not fabricated.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of kulfi is one stick or small bowl weighing approximately 80–120 g; 100 g is used as the reference serving for nutritional calculations.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Ice creams, rich, vanilla (SR Legacy 19095) and full-fat milk components; cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) for kulfi composition estimates
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact kulfi entry exists in USDA FoodData Central. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy ice cream (rich vanilla, 19095) and full-fat milk reduction data, cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN published composition ranges for kulfi-type frozen dairy desserts. Input values (energy 250 kcal, protein 6 g, fat 15 g, carbohydrate 22 g, sugar 20 g, sodium 70 mg, dietary fibre 0.5 g, calcium 180 mg, iron 0.2 mg, cholesterol 45 mg, saturated fat 9 g, trans fat 0.5 g, vitamin C 0.5 mg per 100 g) are consistent with the adapted source range and are retained. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and several micronutrients are set to -1 as detailed fatty acid and full micronutrient profiles for traditional kulfi are not reported in USDA FoodData Central or the ICMR-NIN database.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; analogous estimate based on full-fat ice cream GI (61) as closest approved-source equivalent to kulfi
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for kulfi found in the University of Sydney GI Database. Full-fat ice cream (GI 61, medium) is used as the closest analogous frozen dairy dessert. Kulfi is denser and higher in sugar than standard ice cream, so GI 61 is a conservative estimate; actual GI may be marginally higher. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (61 × 22.0) / 100 = 13.4 per 100 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar (20 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (0.5 g/100 g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention; saturated fat (9 g/100 g) contributes to arterial stiffness
hypertension.guidance: Despite a relatively low sodium content of 70 mg/100 g, the high sugar load (20 g/100 g) and saturated fat (9 g/100 g) independently elevate blood pressure risk through insulin-mediated sodium retention and endothelial dysfunction; individuals with hypertension should limit kulfi to occasional very small portions and prioritise lower-sugar dairy alternatives.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 20 g/100 g at the hard diabetes threshold with negligible fibre (0.5 g/100 g) providing no glucose modulation; medium GI (61) with glycemic load of 13.4 per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Kulfi delivers 20 g of sugar per 100 g with only 0.5 g of dietary fibre, producing a rapid postprandial glucose spike; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid regular consumption and, if consumed at all, restrict to a very small portion (≤50 g) on an infrequent basis while monitoring blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 9 g/100 g (exceeds 5 g/100 g threshold) and trans fat at 0.5 g/100 g raise LDL cholesterol; cholesterol content 45 mg/100 g; high sugar promotes triglyceride synthesis
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of saturated fat (9 g/100 g), trans fat (0.5 g/100 g), and high sugar (20 g/100 g) in kulfi raises LDL cholesterol and triglycerides, both established cardiovascular risk factors; individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should treat kulfi as an occasional indulgence and keep portions to 50 g or less.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat (9 g/100 g) exceeds the 5 g/100 g pro-inflammatory threshold; sugar (20 g/100 g) promotes inflammatory mediators including TNF-alpha and IL-6
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Kulfi's high saturated fat and sugar content can exacerbate systemic inflammation in rheumatoid arthritis; consumption should be avoided during active flares and limited to very small portions during remission.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kulfi is a traditional South Asian frozen dairy dessert originating from the Indian subcontinent, widely consumed across India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, and in South Asian diaspora communities including Singapore. Unlike Western ice cream, kulfi is made by slowly simmering full-fat milk and cream over low heat until the volume reduces by roughly half, concentrating the milk solids, fat, and natural sugars. Sweetened sugar and flavourings such as cardamom, saffron, rose water, pistachios, or almonds are then added before the mixture is poured into conical or cylindrical moulds and frozen without churning. The absence of churning gives kulfi its characteristic dense, firm, and intensely creamy texture that is distinctly richer and less airy than conventional ice cream. Kulfi is typically served on a stick (kulfi on a stick), in a small earthenware cup (matka kulfi), or sliced and plated, often garnished with vermicelli noodles (falooda kulfi). Nutritionally, per 100 g kulfi provides approximately 250 kcal, 6 g protein, 15 g fat (of which 9 g is saturated), 22 g carbohydrate, and 20 g sugar, with a moderate calcium content of 180 mg. The high saturated fat and sugar content make kulfi a high-concern food for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 61 (medium) based on full-fat ice cream as the closest analogous approved-source food, yielding a glycemic load of approximately 13.4 per 100 g serving. Kulfi is halal, vegetarian, and gluten-free in its traditional formulation, though nut-containing variants require allergen awareness for tree-nut-sensitive individuals.
common_user_queries: Is kulfi the same as ice cream?
What is the glycemic index of kulfi?
What flavours does kulfi come in?
What is the difference between kulfi and ice cream?
How much fat is in kulfi?

confidence_reason: No exact kulfi entry exists in USDA FoodData Central or the Singapore HPB database. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy rich vanilla ice cream (19095) and full-fat milk reduction data, cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN published composition ranges. Input values from the source record are consistent with this adapted range and have been retained. GI is an analogous estimate based on full-fat ice cream from the University of Sydney GI Database. Medium confidence is appropriate given the absence of a direct approved-source match.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for traditional kulfi are not available in USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy 19095) or the ICMR-NIN database as of 2026. 2. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for kulfi-type frozen dairy desserts are not consistently reported in the approved sources consulted. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: full micronutrient and moisture profiles for traditional kulfi are not reported in USDA FoodData Central or ICMR-NIN database as of 2026. 4. The ethnic_tag 'Butter Chicken' present in the input cultural field has been removed as it is erroneous for a kulfi record. 5. country_of_origin in the input was listed as Singapore; this has been corrected to Indian Subcontinent as the region of origin, reflecting kulfi's historical and cultural provenance; Singapore is captured as a serving context in meal_context. 6. GI of 61 is an analogous estimate based on full-fat ice cream; actual kulfi GI may be marginally higher due to higher sugar concentration and denser milk-solid matrix. 7. Trans fat value of 0.5 g/100 g is retained from the input; this is plausible for full-fat dairy products containing naturally occurring trans fats (vaccenic acid, conjugated linoleic acid) from ruminant milk fat.
